narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 39 and an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, you've put together a competitive profile in many ways, including coming from a middle-income family and planning to attend full-time. However, the university's admission model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 56.7%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction is based on patterns from thousands of past students, focusing on key indicators of bar exam success like academic rankings and test performance. The most influential factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the typical student's rank of around the 5th or 6th decile, signaling to the model that your later undergraduate performance might not translate well to the rigors of law school and the bar. Next in importance, your strong LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward success since it's above the average of 36.7 and shows real aptitude for the kind of reasoning the bar demands. Your undergraduate GPA also helped by nudging things positively, though at 3.10 it's slightly below the average of 3.21, providing some reassurance of your overall academic capability. These top three elements—your Year 3 decile as the biggest concern, offset by your LSAT and GPA—together tipped the scales toward rejection, as the model prioritizes students whose profiles align more closely with consistent high performers who pass the bar first time. Compared to typical admitted students, your lower decile stands out as a red flag, even with your strengths elsewhere. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to boost your later academic deciles, as schools often re-evaluate with fresh evidence of improvement. You might also gather strong letters of recommendation highlighting your LSAT prowess and full-time commitment, or target schools with holistic reviews that weigh personal growth. Retaking the LSAT isn't necessary given your score, but building a narrative around overcoming that Year 3 dip could make your next application compelling. With targeted steps, you can reshape how models like this see your potential.
